posts.text:
I wrote about tokenmaxxing, the new status game taking over Silicon Valley.

nytimes.com/2026/03/20/techn…

One OpenAI employee used 210 billion tokens *last week*. 

A single Claude Code user spent $150,000 in a month. 

Meta, Shopify and other companies now factor token use into performance reviews.

Are you tokenmaxxing, anon?

Talking to tokenmaxxers for this column gave me a strong suspicion that AI providers are going to be compute-constrained for the foreseeable future. There isn't nearly enough compute in the world for even 1% of white-collar workers to work this way.

It's Hard Fork Friday! This week on the show:

• Are tech layoffs really about AI?
• @jasminewsun tells us why LLMs are bad at writing
• Tokenmaxxing and its discontents 

nytimes.com/2026/03/20/podca…
calling for a 6-month pause on AI journalism until we can overcome urgent alignment challenges such as "are EA and rationalism the same thing?" and "is Gary Marcus a credible source?"

Weirdly, inventing a fake CEO interview was only the second most dubious reporting tactic in this story!


Eugenia Kuyda (@ekuyda)

we've reached peak journalism: Vanity Fair wanted to write a feature story about Dario but never got an interview so just asked Claude to make it up





— https://nitter.net/ekuyda/status/2034385319605477630#m

“The Metaverse” was a 5-year experiment conducted by Harvard University for the purpose of transferring wealth from Meta shareholders to employees who pretended it was fun to go to meetings in a VR headset. We thank you for your participation.


CNBC (@CNBC)

Meta is shutting down VR social platform Horizon Worlds in further pivot away from the metaverse cnbc.com/2026/03/18/meta-hor…




— https://nitter.net/CNBC/status/2034289878431654111#m
If you’re an engineer (or vibecoder) who uses billions of AI tokens a week, DM me. I want to ask you something.

Imagine spending all that money to hire researchers, and you still have to license Gemini.


Andrew Curran (@AndrewCurran_)

META has delayed the release of Avocado until at least May after it underperformed on internal evals, according to reporting by the NYT. They are considering licensing Gemini from Google as a temporary solution.





— https://nitter.net/AndrewCurran_/status/2032246995549896940#m
on the plus side Meta continuing to be bad at AI has substantially lowered my p(doom)

In the 1930s, when rural areas of the US were getting electricity for the first time, the REA would organize “kerosene funerals” where they’d bury an oil lamp and give eulogies to symbolize the passing of an obsolete technology. 

If the AI companies want to improve their approval ratings they should do funerals for expense reports and Jira tickets. Hold a party! Throw a parade! Make people feel something other than dread!
(This post brought to you by my recent re-reading of David Nye’s Electrifying America, a great book) a.co/d/0am51sM5
judging from responses to the AI vs. human writing quiz, twitter appears to be in the bargaining/depression stage of the kubler-ross process, while bluesky is firmly in the denial/anger stage.

We made a blind taste test to see whether NYT readers prefer human writing or AI writing. 

86,000 people have taken it so far, and the results are fascinating. Overall, 54% of quiz-takers prefer AI. A real moment!

nytimes.com/interactive/2026…
To be more precise, 54% of all votes cast were for AI-written passages. The gap on certain passages is wider.

new labor market mnemonic: 

job's in the red, it's dead
job's in the blue, join a construction crew


Tanay Jaipuria (@tanayj)

Nice chart from Anthropic's study on impact of AI on labor markets.

Blue shows theoretical capability of AI (% of tasks) in a job function and red shows observed usage. 

Primarily being adopted in SWE, math, legal, Sales, business/finance work so far but nowhere to the extent it can be.





— https://nitter.net/tanayj/status/2029686529396818153#m
